1: bereshit bara elohim (bĕrēʾšît bārāʾ ʾĕlōhîm): 'In the beginning God created.' Three initial philological observations: (1) bereshit (in the beginning) is in construct state — the absolute beginning, not 'in a beginning of'; (2) bara (created) is used in the OT exclusively with God as subject and without specification of material — implying creation from nothing (ex nihilo), not merely formation from pre-existing chaos; (3) Elohim is grammatically plural but takes a singular verb (bara) — the plural of majesty, or early evidence of the complexity that later theology would develop as Trinity. The first word of Scripture establishes the fundamental theological axiom: God, not chaos, not a pantheon, not nature, is the origin of all things.
26: naase adam betsalmenu kidmutenu (naʿăśeh ʾādām bĕṣalmēnû kidmûtēnû): 'Let us make humanity in our image, after our likeness.' The divine plural (naase, let us; tsalmenu, our image) has generated three interpretive traditions: (1) a plural of majesty; (2) God consulting the heavenly court of angels (cf. Job 1:6; Isa 6:8); (3) an early intra-divine address (Trinitarian foreshadowing, as the Church Fathers read it). Tselem (image) is the word used for cult-statues representing a deity — in ancient Near Eastern thought, the king was the tselem of the gods, their representative and regent. By applying this to all humanity, Genesis democratizes the royal-priestly image: every human, not just the king, represents the divine presence on earth and exercises dominion on behalf of the divine.
2:
תֹהוּ וָבֹהוּ (tōhû wābōhû) — an alliterative, rhyming word-pair meaning "formless and empty" or "unformed and uninhabited." Neither word denotes chaos or destruction in itself; together they describe a state prior to ordered, inhabited creation. The phrase recurs in Jer 4:23 applied to desolated land — showing the pair describes the undoing of ordered creation, not primordial evil.

רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים (rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm) — rûaḥ spans wind, breath, and spirit; the context tips toward the personal divine agent because of mĕraḥepet (hovering/brooding), a Piel participle from a root used of a bird caring for her young (Deut 32:11). Wind does not hover in this deliberate, nurturing sense; the personal Spirit who will animate creation is already present over the unformed deep.

תְּהוֹם (tĕhôm) — "the deep," cognate with Babylonian Tiamat. Unlike Babylonian cosmogony, the deep here is not a monster to be slain but a neutral condition over which God acts freely. The absence of a creation-combat myth is itself a theological statement.
3:
וַיֹּאמֶר אֱלֹהִים — the waw-consecutive imperfect (wayyiqtol) inaugurates the creation narrative's sequence. Hebrew narrative past tense uses this form throughout ch 1: each divine speech and act is rendered sequentially by waw + imperfect, not by simple perfects. The effect is a cascade of completed actions in real time.

יְהִי אוֹר (yĕhî ʾôr) — the jussive "let there be light." Light is created before the luminaries of day 4 — the light of day 1 is not solar light. The separation of the light-source from the light-bearing objects prevents any identification of אוֹר with the sun, important given Babylonian sun-worship. The Hebrew makes no further comment; the gap is theologically loaded.
4:
כִּי-טוֹב (kî-ṭôb) — "that it was good." The kî introduces a direct object clause: God saw that. ṭôb is an aesthetic-moral-functional term — good in the sense of fitting, right, beautiful, and working as intended. Each evaluation unit closes with this phrase; the cumulative effect is a God who evaluates rather than merely produces.

וַיַּבְדֵּל (wayyabdēl) — "and he separated." The verb bādal (to divide, distinguish) is used in Levitical purity law for holy-versus-common distinctions. God's creative work is structuring by distinction; the same verb will describe the Levites' separating role (Num 16:9) and Israel's call to distinguish between clean and unclean.
5:
וַיִּקְרָא (wayyiqrāʾ) — "and he called/named." In the ANE, naming is an act of authority and definition — to name is to establish the nature and role of a thing. God names the light "Day" and darkness "Night"; Adam will later name the animals (2:19-20), exercising derivative dominion through the same act.

יוֹם אֶחָד (yôm ʾeḥāḏ) — "one day" (cardinal, not "first day" as in the ordinal forms used for days 2–6). The distinction may signal that the first day stands apart as the foundation; alternatively, the cardinal signals the day is complete in itself. The evening-morning sequence (evening first) follows the Hebrew reckoning of a day starting at sundown.
6: רָקִיעַ (rāqîaʿ) — from rāqaʿ (to beat out, spread thin — used of hammering metal into sheets, Exod 39:3). The term describes a stretched-out expanse rather than a solid vault; the cognate Akkadian concept does imply a firmament, but the Hebrew verbal root emphasizes extension and spreading rather than rigid solidity. The MKT renders "expanse" rather than "firmament" to avoid reading in a cosmological theory the text does not require.
7: The distinction between bārāʾ (create, v. 1, 21, 27) and ʿāśâ (make, v. 7, 16, 25) is not absolute — both are used of God's creative acts — but bārāʾ appears at key junctures (creation itself, sea creatures and birds, humanity) while ʿāśâ covers the making of the expanse, the luminaries, and land animals. The pattern may signal that bārāʾ marks the truly novel; ʿāśâ covers formation and arrangement of existing material.
8: The repeated wayyĕhî-ʿereb wayyĕhî-bōqer ("and there was evening and there was morning") functions as a boundary marker closing each creation day. The waw-consecutive forms (wayyĕhî) keep the narrative moving — these are not isolated static descriptions but links in a temporal chain. The formula's regularity creates an architectural rhythm that the seventh day (2:1-3) will deliberately break.
9:
יִקָּווּ (yiqqāwû) — Niphal imperfect of qāwāh (to collect, wait). The waters gather themselves by divine command — the passive (Niphal) voice emphasizes divine causation without direct physical intervention; God speaks and reality conforms.

תֵּרָאֶה (tērāʾeh) — Niphal imperfect, "let the dry ground appear" — again the passive of appearance by command. The land does not so much emerge as become visible as the waters comply. The same verb will describe theophany: the LORD appears (nîrāʾ) to the patriarchs.
10: God names the dry ground אֶרֶץ (ʾereṣ, "land/earth") and the gathered waters יַמִּים (yammîm, "seas"). The naming authority exercised in v. 5 continues. The verb wayyiqrāʾ is the same throughout — naming as the royal act of defining and governing what one names.
11: לְמִינֵהוּ (lĕmînēhû) — "according to its kind." The phrase appears ten times in ch 1 and establishes the boundaries of reproducible kinds. The Hebrew mîn does not map exactly onto any modern biological category; it describes reproducibility within a kind. The phrase is not a statement about species boundaries but about the orderliness of creation — vegetation reproduces what it is.
12: The land "brought forth" (waṯṣēʾ hāʾāreṣ) in response to God's word — the ground is an agent executing the divine command, not merely a passive substrate. This mediated creation (God speaks → earth produces) is the pattern for the sea creatures (v. 20), land animals (v. 24), and eventually humanity (2:7, where God acts more directly). Each creative domain has its own mode of response.
13: The evening-morning formula closes the third day. Note that days 1-3 (separation: light/dark, waters/waters, water/land) are structurally paired with days 4-6 (filling: luminaries, birds/fish, land animals/humanity). Day 7 stands outside the pair. This literary architecture is a feature of the Hebrew, not translatable; it signals the text's compositional intentionality.
14: אֹתֹת וּמוֹעֲדִים (ʾōtōt ûmôʿăḏîm) — "signs and appointed times." Môʿēd (pl. môʿăḏîm) is the word used for Israel's appointed feasts (Lev 23:2-4) — the sacred calendar. The luminaries are set in the sky to govern the calendar, including the religious calendar; the creation of timekeeping is intrinsically tied to the ordering of worship. This verse is the theological basis for Sabbath and festival reckoning from the creation order.
15: The purpose clause — lĕhāʾîr ʿal-hāʾāreṣ ("to give light on the earth") — specifies function over essence. The text is not interested in the physics of luminaries but in their teleological role as servants of God's ordered creation. They are made (ʿāśâ) and placed (nātan) in service of the earth below.
16: שְׁנֵי הַמְּאֹרֹת הַגְּדֹלִים (šĕnê hammĕʾōrōt haggĕḏōlîm) — "the two great lights." Notably, the sun and moon are not named in the Hebrew text. The surrounding cultures had names — Shamash (sun), Sin (moon) — that carried divine status. By calling them only "the greater light" and "the lesser light," the text demotes them from deities to instruments. The stars are added almost as an afterthought ("and the stars also"), further reducing their cultic significance.
17: God "set them" (wayyittēn) in the expanse — the verb nātan (give, set, place) is used here as a functional placement. The luminaries are placed in a location for a purpose; they have no independent will or divine status. The same word describes giving gifts, placing objects, and assigning roles — here the luminaries are assigned servants, not worshipped lords.
18: The evaluation kî-ṭôb follows the luminaries' commission. Three functions are named: governing day and night, separating light from darkness. The threefold statement of purpose before the evaluation recalls the pattern of each creation unit — the goodness is tied to the fitness of the thing for its appointed role.
19: The evening-morning formula (fourth day). The structural pair with day 1 (light/dark separation) is now complete: day 1 created the condition, day 4 created the governors of that condition. The Hebrew architecture is clearer than any translation: days 1-3 create realms; days 4-6 fill them in corresponding order.
20:
יִשְׁרְצוּ (yišrĕṣû) — "let them swarm/teem." The verb šāraṣ denotes swarming, teeming life — used of small creatures in masses (insects, fish, frogs; cf. Exod 8:3; Lev 11:29). The same root gives šereṣ (swarming things), a major category of Levitical impurity. The use of the verb here is simply about abundance; the later purity connotations are applied differently.

עוֹף (ʿôp) — "flying creatures," not strictly "birds." The root ʿûp means to fly; the category includes all that flies and is broader than modern "birds." The MKT's "birds" approximates; the Hebrew does not exclude flying insects.
21:
וַיִּבְרָא — bārāʾ reappears here (day 5) after being absent since v. 1. The reuse marks sea creatures and birds as a category requiring a special creative act — not just forming or making but bārāʾ. The pattern will repeat at v. 27 for humanity. These three bārāʾ uses may mark the three most significant creative boundaries.

הַתַּנִּינִם הַגְּדֹלִים (hattannînim haggĕḏōlîm) — "the great sea creatures." The same word (tannîn) is used elsewhere for the sea-monster Leviathan (Ps 74:13; Isa 27:1). Here it is demythologized: the great sea creatures are made things, not divine adversaries. They are created by God and evaluated as ṭôb.
22: וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתָם אֱלֹהִים — the first divine blessing in Genesis. The blessing formula (bārak) confers fruitfulness and multiplication. The same blessing will be given to humanity (1:28) and later to Noah (9:1). The blessing is functional — it enables and capacitates the creatures to fill their domains. The sea creatures and birds are blessed before any command is given to them beyond reproduction.
23: Fifth day formula closes. The architecture of the creation narrative requires that sea/sky (day 5) correspond to the separation of waters/sky (day 2). The filling of the sea and sky is now complete; day 6 will fill the land corresponding to day 3's land/sea distinction.
24: נֶפֶשׁ חַיָּה (nepeš ḥayyâ) — "living creatures/souls." The same phrase will describe the human being at 2:7. Nepeš is the animated, living being — not an immaterial soul but the whole creature in its vitality. Land animals and humanity share this description; the distinction comes in how they receive it (ground-production for animals; divine formation and breath for humanity).
25: The kî-ṭôb evaluation for land animals. All three categories — ḥayyat hāʾāreṣ (wild beasts), bĕhēmāh (livestock), and remeś (creeping things) — are evaluated good individually in the Hebrew. The MKT's "livestock" for bĕhēmāh captures the domesticated-animal sense (cattle, sheep, donkeys).
27:
וַיִּבְרָא אֱלֹהִים אֶת-הָאָדָם בְּצַלְמוֹ — bārāʾ appears three times in this verse: "God created mankind... in his image he created him; male and female he created them." The threefold repetition is poetic (this is one of the few poetic couplets/triplets in the creation narrative) and emphatic. The third bārāʾ expands from the individual to the pair: zākār ûnĕqēbāh (male and female), both are the ṣelem of God, both are created (bārāʾ) — not derived sequentially as in ch 2 but co-created as image-bearers together.

אָדָם (ʾāḏām) — in ch 1 this is the generic term for humankind (collective), not the proper name Adam. The semantic range covers: mankind collectively, a human being, and the proper name Adam (used in ch 5). The MKT renders "mankind" in ch 1 to preserve the collective sense; the shift to the proper name occurs at 2:20 and especially 3:17.
28:
וַיְבָרֶךְ אֹתָם — the blessing precedes the mandate: God blesses before commanding. The fertility command (pĕrû ûrĕbû, be fruitful and multiply) is not a law but the activation of a capacity already given in the blessing. The same formula appears at Noah's re-commissioning (9:1, 7) and the Abrahamic promises — the creation mandate is renewed after each catastrophic disruption.

כִּבְשֻׁהָ (kibbĕšûhā) — "subdue it." Kābaš is a strong term (tread down, subdue, bring into subjection — used of subduing enemies, Num 32:22, 29; Josh 18:1). Its use for humanity's relationship to the earth is intentionally robust — the mandate is not gentle management but active, purposeful ordering. This is royal-administrative language, not merely gardening.
29: זֹרֵעַ זֶרַע (zōrēaʿ zeraʿ) — "seed-bearing," a participial phrase modifying plants. The preoccupation with seed-bearing points forward to the seed as the mechanism of continuity — the same word zeraʿ will carry the promise of Gen 3:15. In ch 1 it is simply agricultural; its recurrence in 3:15, 12:7, 13:15, 15:3-5, 22:17-18 shows the seed motif gathering theological weight.
30: The extension of plant-food to all living creatures (nepeš ḥayyâ) in the original creation establishes a pre-Fall order without predation. The absence of meat-eating in the original mandate (reversed at 9:3 after the Flood) is a textual fact that the Hebrew presents without explicit comment. The kî kēn ("and it was so") formula closes with simple compliance.
31: מְאֹד (mĕʾōḏ) — "very." The single adverb elevates the cumulative evaluation beyond each day's ṭôb: all that he had made is not just good but emphatically, thoroughly good. This is the only use of mĕʾōḏ with ṭôb in the creation account — the final assessment is heightened over all partial ones. The sixth day closes the pairing with day 3 and completes the filling of creation's realms.
